results.title: LLMs Improving LLMs: Agentic Discovery for Test-Time Scaling (May 2026)
results.content:
Summary:
This paper proposes AutoTTS, a framework that automates the discovery of test-time scaling (TTS) strategies for large language models. Instead of manually hand-crafting branching and stopping heuristics, researchers construct environments where an explorer LLM iteratively proposes and refines code-defined controllers. By utilizing an offline replay environment, beta parameterization, and fine-grained execution trace feedback, AutoTTS identifies strategies that outperform traditional baselines in accuracy-cost tradeoffs on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with a total discovery cost of only $39.90.

Key Topics:
- Test-Time Scaling (TTS)
- Large Language Models
- Agentic Discovery
- Controller Synthesis
- Mathematical Reasoning
- Inference Efficiency
- AutoML [...] LLM agent, specifically Claude code, automatically discovers optimal scaling
strategies. >> By by writing raw Python code in an
offline replay environment. >> Right. And the results are just wild.
For exactly $39.90 and like 160 minutes of wall clock time,
this agent discovered a new controller. >> A controller that absolutely crushes
human baselines like self-consistency and parallel probe. Yeah, it cuts token
cost by nearly 70% on math benchmarks while maintaining accuracy. And
incredibly, it generalizes to entirely different model families.
>> It's a massive shift. >> Okay, so that's the summary. But for the
rest of this deep dive into the source material, our mission is to really
unpack exactly how this $40 agent outsmarted human researchers. [...] # LLMs Improving LLMs: Agentic Discovery for Test-Time Scaling (May 2026)

MIND LAB INTRODUCES MINT FOR SCALING LORA TO MILLIONS OF LLMS

Mind Lab introduced MinT last week, a managed infrastructure toolkit designed to streamline Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) training and serving. The system keeps base models resident in memory while moving only compact adapter revisions to hide the complexity of distributed training.

AI ResearchJune 4, 2026

# 3x Faster Search: Parallel Test-Time Scaling with Instructed-Retriever-1 [...] by The Databricks AI Research Team

Today we’re announcing a major update that makes Agent Bricks Knowledge Assistant both faster and higher quality. Answer generation time has dropped by 2x, and search time has dropped by more than 3x, bringing Time To First Token (TTFT) to around two seconds.¹ Thus, Knowledge Assistant users will get noticeably faster answers across their use cases, with no reconfiguration required and no tradeoff in quality. [...] ## Conclusion

This update brings Parallel Test-Time Scaling into the production search stack. The system retrieves broadly through parallel query and filter generation, then reranks precisely with multi-pivot evidence comparison. Instructed-Retriever-1 powers both stages with a single retrieval-specialized model trained for search generation and evidence ranking. The result is a Knowledge Assistant that is both better and faster: search time drops by more than 3x, answer generation time drops by 2x, TTFT is around 2s, and end-to-end latency is consistently below 10s on our offline eval setup.¹

results.title: When and How Much to Imagine: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling with World Models for Visual Spatial Reasoning | OpenReview
results.content:
back arrowBack to the profile of Huaxiu Yao

## When and How Much to Imagine: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling with World Models for Visual Spatial Reasoning

Download PDF

### Shoubin Yu, Yue Zhang, Zun Wang, Jaehong Yoon, Huaxiu Yao, Mingyu Ding, Mohit Bansal

Published: 27 May 2026, Last Modified: 01 Jun 2026FMEA @ CVPR 2026 PosterEveryoneRevisionsBibTeXCC BY 4.0

Keywords: Spatial Reasoning; World Model; Test-time Scaling; MLLM; Embodied Reasoning [...] reveal clear scenarios where imagination is critical, marginal, or detrimental, and show that selective control can match or outperform fixed imagination strategies with substantially fewer world-model calls and language tokens. Our AVIC-R surpasses strong proprietary baselines including GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 while invoking the WM less often. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of analyzing and controlling test-time imagination for efficient and reliable spatial reasoning. paper under peer review. [...] when static visual evidence is sufficient, when imagination improves reasoning, and how excessive or unnecessary imagination affects accuracy and efficiency. To support this analysis, we then introduce AVIC, an adaptive test-time framework with world models that explicitly reasons about the sufficiency of current visual evidence before selectively invoking and scaling visual imagination. Finally, to further learn this gating and planning behavior without any annotation of when and how much to imagine, we introduce AVIC-R, which trains the policy end-to-end via GRPO from QA-correctness rewards and penalties by imagination cost. Across spatial reasoning benchmarks (SAT, MMSI) and an embodied navigation benchmark (R2R), our results reveal clear scenarios where imagination is critical,

results.title: ThinkBooster: A Unified Framework for Seamless Test-Time Scaling ...
results.content:
Common TTC scaling strategies include best-of-NN (BoN: Cobbe et al. (2021); Snell et al. (2025)), tree-of-thought (ToT: Yao et al. (2023), see Figure˜1), and self-consistency (also known as majority voting: Wang et al. (2023)).
Recently, there have been a number of proposed dynamic TTC scaling strategies that adapt the compute spent based on confidence or uncertainty estimated at the level of individual reasoning steps Zhang et al. (2025a); Fu et al. (2026); Yan et al. (2025). [...] It has also been shown that increasing compute at test time, such as by generating longer reasoning chains or sampling multiple solutions and selecting the best ones, can significantly improve performance on challenging problems. This approach is known as test-time compute (TTC) scaling Snell et al. (2025); Muennighoff et al. (2025).
It is especially effective when a state-of-the-art LLM fails to solve a challenging problem in a single pass and cannot be further fine-tuned. Moreover, recent work shows that TTC scaling can provide a better performance to efficiency trade-off than simply increasing model size Snell et al. (2025). [...] Test-time compute (TTC) scaling has emerged as a powerful paradigm for improving large language model (LLM) reasoning by allocating additional compute during inference, e.g., via multi-sample generation and verifier-based reranking. Existing TTC scaling strategies and reasoning scorers remain fragmented, evaluated under inconsistent protocols, and are rarely analyzed through the lens of quality-cost trade-offs. We introduce ThinkBooster, a unified framework for seamless test-time compute scaling of LLM reasoning, which consists of (i) a modular Python library implementing state-of-the-art TTC scaling strategy and scorer families,

There’s a LOT packed into Test of Time, more than can be captured in a single set of patch notes, and we think it’s best experienced by booting up a new game. From staying as one civ throughout your campaign, to the new Triumphs system replacing Legacy Paths, to a full rework of Victories, this update changes that way building your empire will feel and play out in pretty major ways.

There’s still more ahead for Civilization VII, but for now: pick your favorite civ, start a fresh campaign, and ask yourself one important question: can you stand the test of time? [...] Most underrated civilizations in Civ VI

results.title: LLM Scaling Laws: Analysis from AI Researchers - AIMultiple
results.content:
### “Scaling Behaviors of LLM Reinforcement Learning Post-Training”, Zhang, Yin et al.

The Scaling Behaviors of LLM Reinforcement Learning Post-Training (2026) paper examines how reinforcement learning during post-training scales with model size and compute.

The authors conduct an empirical study on models of varying scales and find that the relationship between test loss, compute, and data can be modeled using a predictive power law. This pattern holds across both base and instruction-tuned models, suggesting that reinforcement learning post-training follows regularities similar to those observed in pretraining. [...] | Paper | Year | What is being scaled | Optimization objective | Key finding | Practical implication |
 ---  ---  --- |
| Scaling Behaviors of LLM RL Post-Training | 2026 | Model size, RL compute, RL data | Predict reasoning performance from RL post-training | RL post-training follows a power law with latent saturation | Reinforcement learning scaling has built-in limits beyond pretraining |
| Scaling Laws Meet Model Architecture | 2025 | Parameters, training tokens, architecture | Jointly optimize accuracy and inference cost | Architectural choices shift the scaling curve | Hidden size and attention design should be tuned alongside scale | [...] Sıla Ermut

updated on May 22, 2026

See ourethical norms

Large language models predict the next token based on patterns learned from text data. The term LLM scaling laws refers to empirical regularities that link model performance to the amount of compute, training data, and model parameters used during training.

To understand how these relationships influence modern model design in practice, we reviewed findings from 8 academic papers and insights from 3 major AI labs and researchers.

## Key takeaways from LLM scaling laws analysis

Leading researchers converge on the following key insights:

results.title: Orals - ICML 2026
results.content: Scaling large models requires optimization strategies that ensure rapid convergence grounded in stability. Maximal Update Parametrization ($\boldsymbol{\mu}$P) provides a theoretical safeguard for width-invariant $\Theta(1)$ activation control, whereas emerging optimizers like Muon are only "half-aligned" with these constraints: they control updates but allow weights to drift. To address this limitation, we introduce the \\Spectral Sphere Optimizer (SSO)\\, which enforces strict module-wise spectral constraints on both weights and their updates. By deriving the steepest descent direction on the spectral sphere, SSO realizes a fully $\boldsymbol{\mu}$P-aligned optimization process. To enable large‑scale training, we implement SSO as an efficient parallel algorithm within Megatron. Through [...] Reinforcement learning (RL), particularly RL from verifiable reward (RLVR), has become a crucial phase of training large language models (LLMs) and a key focus of current scaling efforts. However, optimization practices in RL largely follow those of next-token-prediction stages (e.g., pretraining and supervised fine-tuning), despite the fundamental differences between RL and these stages emphasized by recent work. One such practice is the use of the AdamW optimizer, which is widely adopted for training large-scale transformers despite its high memory overhead. Our analysis shows that both momentum and adaptive learning rate of AdamW are less influential in RL than in SFT, leading us to hypothesize that RL benefits less from Adam’s per-parameter adaptive learning rates and momentum. [...] Genomic foundation models have the potential to decode DNA syntax, yet face a fundamental tradeoff. Standard subword tokenizers fragment biologically meaningful motifs such as codons and regulatory elements, while nucleotide-level models preserve biological coherence but incur prohibitive computational costs for long contexts. We introduce dnaHNet, a state-of-the-art tokenizer-free autoregressive model that segments and models genomic sequences end to end. Using a differentiable dynamic chunking mechanism, dnaHNet compresses raw nucleotides into latent tokens adaptively, balancing compression with predictive accuracy. Pretrained on prokaryotic genomes, dnaHNet outperforms leading architectures including StripedHyena2 in scaling and efficiency. This recursive chunking yields quadratic FLOP
